note: Representative values for educational use. Actual performance varies with engine design.

propellants.name: LOX / RP-1
propellants.description: Workhorse combination used by Falcon 9, Atlas V, and Antares. High density, good Isp, cryogenic LOX.

educational_notes.pros: High density reduces tank volume; RP-1 is non-cryogenic (simplifies ground ops)
educational_notes.cons: Carbon deposition in engines limits reuse without refurbishment; lower Isp than LH2

propellants.name: LOX / LH2
propellants.description: Highest Isp liquid combination. Used by Space Shuttle main engines, RS-68, RL-10.

educational_notes.pros: Highest vacuum Isp (~450 s) of practical propellants
educational_notes.cons: LH2 has very low density (71 kg/m³) → huge tanks; both propellants cryogenic

propellants.name: LOX / Methane
propellants.description: Next-generation propellant used by SpaceX Raptor (Starship) and Blue Origin BE-4 (New Glenn).

educational_notes.pros: Better Isp than RP-1; cleaner combustion improves reuse; potentially available on Mars
educational_notes.cons: Cryogenic fuel; lower density than RP-1 increases tank volume

propellants.name: N₂O₄ / UDMH
propellants.description: Hypergolic storable propellants. Used by Proton, Long March, and many upper stages.

educational_notes.pros: Storable at room temperature; hypergolic (ignite on contact, no igniter needed)
educational_notes.cons: Highly toxic; lower Isp than cryogenic options; environmental concerns
